1	{ "prompt": "Construct a Meridian Academic Publishing & Fulfillment Portal supporting offline-first account access for students, instructors, academic staff, and administrators who manage academic content submissions, reviews, and physical issue fulfillment from a single web experience. The Dioxus-based UI presents a role-aware dashboard after local username/password login, with clear navigation to User Center, Orders, Submissions, and After-Sales. Users can edit profiles, manage US shipping addresses with a single default address, set invoice titles for printable receipts, and control notification preferences limited to in-app inbox banners (email/SMS options appear but remain disabled in offline mode). Students and instructors create multi-round submissions using guided templates (forms plus file attachments), can resubmit up to 10 times before the stated deadline, and always see version history with timestamps in MM/DD/YYYY and 12-hour time; downloads apply visible watermarks showing the requester’s name and time. Order & fulfillment screens support subscription periods (monthly, quarterly, annual), line-item quantities, split/merge when an order contains multiple series, and a reconciliation view that compares expected vs. actual receipt per issue, logging missing issues, reshipments, delays, discontinuations, and edition changes with a required reason. Reviews and after-sales flows let users post an initial review plus one follow-up within 14 days, attach up to 6 images (5 MB each), and track return/refund/exchange cases through statuses from Submitted to In Review, Awaiting Evidence, Arbitrated, Approved/Denied, and Closed, with clear SLA timers (first response within 2 business days, resolution target 7 business days).
2	The backend uses Rocket to expose REST-style endpoints consumed by the Dioxus frontend over the same local network or localhost, keeping the architecture decoupled while remaining fully offline-runnable. MySQL persists users, roles, permissions, orders, fulfillment events, submission versions, review records, and after-sales cases, with RBAC enforcing menu/button/data-scope access and an auditable trail of permission changes (who changed what and when). Authentication is local-only with salted password hashing and 30-minute idle session expiry; password recovery is handled by admin-issued one-time reset tokens printed or copied in person, expiring after 60 minutes. Account deletion performs a 30-day soft-delete hold to prevent accidental loss, while “Export My Data” generates a local downloadable archive limited to the requester’s records. Content metadata governance runs on save: title/summary/tags/keywords are validated for length limits (title 120 chars, summary 500), SEO fields are generated from existing text using deterministic rules, and sensitive-word detection uses a local dictionary with replace-or-block policies configurable by admins; blocked content enters a review workflow requiring academic staff approval before publication. File handling enforces format allowlists (PDF/DOCX/PNG/JPG), size limits (25 MB per file), and basic safety checks via file signature verification and checksum logging rather than cloud scanning. Payments are implemented as a pluggable offline module: by default the system records “Cash/Check/On-Account” transactions with idempotent posting rules, escrow-style holds, refunds, and nightly reconciliation reports; any third-party payment options are explicitly disabled unless an offline-compatible connector is installed, and abnormal-order interception flags patterns such as unusually high quantities or repeated refunds for manual review.

1	use server::{auth, crypto, db, middleware, routes, AppState};
2	
3	use axum::{
4	    middleware as axum_mw,
5	    routing::{delete, get, post, put},
6	    Router,
7	};
8	use std::sync::Arc;
9	use tower_http::cors::CorsLayer;
10	use tower_http::services::ServeDir;
11	
12	#[tokio::main]
13	async fn main() {
14	    let db_path = std::env::var("DB_PATH")
15	        .unwrap_or_else(|_| "data/fund_transparency.db".to_string());
16	    if let Some(parent) = std::path::Path::new(&db_path).parent() {
17	        std::fs::create_dir_all(parent).ok();
18	    }
19	    let db = db::init_db(&db_path);
20	
21	    // Host-managed key paths — outside repo tree by default
22	    let hmac_path = std::env::var("HMAC_KEY_PATH")
23	        .unwrap_or_else(|_| "/var/lib/fund_transparency/hmac.key".to_string());
24	    let hmac_secret = load_or_create_secret(&hmac_path);
25	    let encryption_key = crypto::load_or_create_key().unwrap_or_else(|e| {
26	        eprintln!("FATAL: {}", e);
27	        std::process::exit(1);
28	    });
29	    let rate_limiter = middleware::RateLimitState::new(60, 60); // 60 requests per 60 seconds
30	
31	    let state = Arc::new(AppState {
32	        db,
33	        hmac_secret,
34	        encryption_key,
35	        rate_limiter,
36	    });
37	
38	    // CORS: restrict to configured origins; fall back to same-origin only in production.
39	    let allowed_origins = std::env::var("CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS")
40	        .unwrap_or_else(|_| "http://localhost:8080,http://127.0.0.1:8080".to_string());
41	    let origins: Vec<axum::http::HeaderValue> = allowed_origins
42	        .split(',')
43	        .filter_map(|o| o.trim().parse().ok())
44	        .collect();
45	    let cors = CorsLayer::new()

160	fn load_or_create_secret(path: &str) -> Vec<u8> {
161	    use std::path::Path;
162	    let p = Path::new(path);
163	    if p.exists() {
164	        std::fs::read(p).expect("Failed to read HMAC key")
165	    } else {
166	        use rand::Rng;
167	        let secret: Vec<u8> = (0..64).map(|_| rand::thread_rng().gen()).collect();
168	        if let Some(parent) = p.parent() {
169	            std::fs::create_dir_all(parent).ok();
170	        }
171	        std::fs::write(p, &secret).expect("Failed to write HMAC key");
172	        secret
173	    }
174	}

Another issue — nginx can't resolve the `backend` hostname. Let me check the nginx config.

The issue is that nginx tries to resolve `backend` at startup. Since `depends_on` with `service_healthy` makes the frontend wait, the backend should be up. But nginx on Alpine resolves hostnames at startup and fails if the DNS isn't ready. The fix is to use a resolver directive with Docker's internal DNS.

The backend API works directly, but the nginx proxy returns 404. The issue is with the `proxy_pass` using a variable — when you use a variable in `proxy_pass`, nginx doesn't automatically append the matched URI portion. Let me check the proxy behavior more carefully.

The problem is that with the `set $backend_upstream` trick, nginx doesn't append the path properly. The `/api/` location captures the request, but the `$backend_upstream/api/` in `proxy_pass` with a variable doesn't strip/replace the prefix correctly. I need to use `$request_uri` or reconstruct the path.
